terms.definition: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or performing a computation.
A collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, accessible by index.
Mathematical notation describing the limiting behavior of a function when the argument tends towards infinity. Used to classify algorithms by how they respond to changes in input size.
A search algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
A tree data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as left and right child.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores vertices in order of their distance from the source vertex.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
An algorithmic paradigm that solves complex problems by breaking them down into simpler subproblems and storing the results of subproblems to avoid computing the same results again.
A collection of vertices (nodes) connected by edges, used to represent relationships between objects.
A data structure that implements an associative array abstract data type using a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets.
A specialized tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property: parent nodes are ordered with respect to their children.
A linear collection of data elements whose order is not given by their physical placement in memory. Each element points to the next.
A linear data structure that follows the First In, First Out (FIFO) principle.
A method of solving problems where the solution depends on solutions to smaller instances of the same problem.
A linear data structure that follows the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle.
The computational complexity that describes the amount of time taken by an algorithm to run as a function of the length of the input.
The amount of memory space an algorithm uses as a function of its input size.
A tree-like data structure used to store a dynamic set of strings, where each node represents a common prefix.
